kh:body.kh:mini-summary: This Practice Note provides an overview of swaps (including interest rate, currency, commodity and credit default swaps), forward rate agreements, futures and options).
kh:body.core:para: The main types of derivatives are:

core:listitem.core:para: swaps
forwards
futures, and
options

kh:body.core:para: This Practice Note explains the key features of each of the above types of derivatives.
For a general introduction to derivatives, see Practice Note: Introductory guide to derivatives.

tr:secmain.core:title: Swaps
tr:secmain.core:para: Swaps: Henderson on Derivatives [2.2]A swap describes a wide variety of derivative transactions. Since their inception in the 1980s, swaps have evolved into a worldwide market encompassing trillions of pounds worldwide in notional value. They are subject to different regulations and laws depending on the market in which they are traded. Swaps are generally documented under the International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) framework. For more information, see Practice Note: Derivatives—ISDA documentation framework).
For more information on why parties enter into swaps, see Practice Note: The nature of financial derivatives—Key uses of derivatives.

In a swap, two parties enter into a contract in which they agree to exchange payment streams. Each party to the swap makes regular payments to the other. The payments made under the swap by one party are calculated on a different basis to the payments made by the other party.

At the beginning of the life of a swap, its net value should, in theory, be zero. During the life of the swap, rates will move and the swap will become an asset to one party and a liability to the other. Once a swap becomes an asset to one party, that party is exposed to the credit risk of the other party.

The majority of swaps are traded over-the-counter (OTC). OTC derivatives are negotiated and traded privately between two parties (one of which is usually a financial institution) and tailored to the needs of the particular parties. They are not traded on exchanges. OTC contracts constitute by far the largest part of the derivatives market.
The key types of swaps are:

core:listitem.core:para: interest rate swaps
currency (rate) swaps
commodity swaps, and
credit default swaps

tr:secsub1.core:title: Interest rate swaps
tr:secsub1.core:para: Interest rate swaps: Henderson on Derivatives [2.4]The most common type of interest rate swap (known as a fixed-for-floating interest rate swap) involves:

core:listitem.core:para: one party making payments by reference to a variable (or floating) rate of interest, eg SONIA, and
the other party making payments based on a fixed rate of interest

tr:secsub1.core:para: In a typical loan transaction, borrowers may enter into interest rate swaps with each other to hedge against the risk of an increase in the interest payments it is making to the lender. For example:

core:listitem.core:para: party A is under an obligation to pay interest at a floating rate of SONIA plus 3% on a loan of £10m to bank A
party B is under an obligation to pay interest at a fixed rate of 5% on a loan of €10m to bank B
party A does not want to take the risk of SONIA increasing and would prefer to pay a fixed rate
party B is prepared to take the risk of SONIA increasing
in effect, party A and party B swap interest rate obligations:

core:sub1listitem.core:para: party A pays regular amounts to party B equal to interest at a fixed rate of 5% on a loan of €10m—party B uses that amount to pay bank B
party B pays regular amounts to party A equal to interest at a floating rate of SONIA plus 3% on a loan of €10m—party A uses that amount to pay bank A

tr:secsub1.core:title: Currency rate swaps

kh:graphic.filename: Currency swap transaction diagram - 1 Initial exchange.png
Currency swap transaction diagram - 2 Periodic payments.png
Currency swap transaction diagram - 3 Final Exchange.png

tr:secsub1.core:para: Currency swaps: Henderson on Derivatives [2.3]Currency products: Henderson on Derivatives [3.3]A typical currency swap transaction involves two parties exchanging amounts in different currencies, the relative amounts being calculated on the basis of an agreed exchange rate. Currency rate swaps are typically used by a party who has an obligation to a third party in one currency but receives its income in a different currency. By entering into a currency rate swap, that party can hedge against the risk of adverse changes in exchange rates, for example:

core:listitem.core:para: party A receives the majority of its income in euros
party A has entered into a Sterling loan with its bank and is obliged to repay the loan in Sterling
party A does not want to take the risk of an unfavourable movement in the Sterling/euro exchange rate which would result in it not having enough income to repay its loan to the bank
party B is prepared to take the risk of movements in the Sterling/euro exchange rate
party A and party B agree to a fixed Sterling/euro exchange rate at the start of the term of party X's loan with its bank:

core:sub1listitem.core:para: party B pays regular amounts to party A in Sterling at the pre-agreed Sterling/euro exchange rate (calculated by reference to the repayment schedule under party X's loan with its bank)—party A uses that amount to repay its Sterling loan
party A uses its euro income to pay equivalent regular amounts to party Y in euros at the pre-agreed Sterling/euro exchange rate

tr:secsub1.core:para: It is a slightly more complicated transaction than an interest rate swap as it typically involves three stages:

core:listitem.core:para: an initial exchange of amounts in different currencies
periodic payments during the life of the transaction in the currency each party received on the initial exchange, and
a final exchange by the parties of the currency amounts reversing the currency exchanged at initial exchange

tr:secsub1.core:para: Currency swaps can also involve interest rate swaps, in which case they are known as cross-currency interest rate swaps. In these swaps, the rate of interest may be a fixed rate for one party and floating rate for the other party. If this is the case, diagram 2 reflecting the periodic payments made during the life of the swap will be amended so that it mirrors an interest rate swap.

tr:secsub1.core:title: Commodity swaps

kh:graphic.filename: Commodity swaps - Payment flows when commodity is above agreed rate.png
Commodity swaps - Payment flows when commodity is below agreed rate of 10.png
Commodity swaps - Payment flows when commodity is at agreed rate of 10.png

tr:secsub1.core:para: Commodity (and other) derivatives: Henderson on Derivatives [3.10]2005 ISDA Commodity (and other) definitions: Henderson on Derivatives [3.11]Commodity transactions: Henderson on Derivatives [3.12]A typical commodity swap involves an agreement between two parties to fix the price of a commodity and to make regular payments to each other based on the difference between the fixed price agreed between the parties and the market price of the commodity. Commodity swaps are used where one party is exposed to fluctuations in commodity prices, either because it has agreed to buy a commodity in the future at the market price and does not want the price to increase or because it is relying on the income from selling the commodity and does not want the price to decrease. By entering into a commodity swap, the risk of an adverse change in commodity prices can be hedged against. For example:

core:listitem.core:para: party A wishes to buy a certain amount of corn per month for the next year
party A is concerned that the price of corn will increase over the next year
party B is prepared to take the risk that the price of corn will increase over the next year
party A and party B agree to a notional fixed price for corn at the start of the year, say, for example £10 per bag of corn (the swap price)
party A purchases corn in the market place throughout the year:

core:sub1listitem.core:para: if party A purchases corn in the market place for more than £10 per bag, party B pays the difference between the swap price and the market price to party A
if party A purchases corn in the market place for less than £10 per bag, party A pays the difference between the swap price and the market price to party B
if party A purchases corn in the marketplace for exactly £10 per bag, no payment is made by either party

core:listitem.core:para: the net effect is that party A always pays £10 per bag for the corn and that party B takes the risk in the fluctuating market price of the corn

tr:secsub1.core:para: For more information about commodity swaps, see Practice Note: Commodity derivatives.

tr:secsub1.core:title: Credit default swaps

kh:graphic.filename: Credit default swap structure diagram - Cash settled.png
Credit default swap structure diagram - Physically delivered.png
Credit default swap structure diagram - Auction settlement.png

tr:secsub1.core:para: Credit default swap: Henderson on Derivatives [5.3]The most common type of credit derivative transaction is a credit default swap (CDS). This is a transaction between two parties which is based on the creditworthiness of a third party, known as the reference entity. The protection buyer is the party that is purchasing the credit protection and the protection seller is the party that is selling that credit protection. A CDS contract is traded OTC and is typically an individually negotiated unfunded contract. The protection buyer will pay a premium to the protection seller, usually by way of periodic payments, in exchange for the protection seller agreeing to bear the credit risk of the underlying reference entity. If a credit event occurs on the underlying reference entity, the protection seller must compensate the protection buyer for its loss by means of a defined settlement procedure. This may be on the basis of auction, cash or physical settlement depending on the terms of the CDS contract.

core:listitem.core:para: the protection seller (party A) and protection buyer (party B) enter into a CDS contract

party B agrees to pay a fee or 'premium' to party A periodically throughout the life of the CDS—this fee is based on the notional value of the CDS, and this is the amount of exposure that party A has agreed to assume
in exchange, party A agrees to make a payment to party B if a credit event occurs on the underlying reference entity

if a credit event does occur, the CDS contract will settle on the basis of auction, cash or physical settlement, depending on what is agreed in the contract and once the CDS contract has settled, the CDS contract will terminate (for more information, see Practice Note: Credit derivatives—settlement procedures)

tr:secsub1.core:para: For more information on credit default swaps, see Practice Note: Credit default swap (CDS) transactions.

tr:secsub1.core:title: Other types of swap
tr:secsub1.core:para: Caps floors and collars: Henderson on Derivatives [2.6]There are a number of other different types of swap that can be entered into including:

core:listitem.core:para: total return swaps (see Practice Notes: Total return swaps and Total return swap (TRS) structure diagram for more information on total return swaps)
Swaptions and options to terminate: Henderson on Derivatives [2.7]swaptions (options on a swap)
zero coupon swaps (for more information on how a zero coupon inflation swap works, see Practice Note: Zero coupon inflation swap structure diagram), and
Forwards and additional leverage: Henderson on Derivatives [7.14]forward rate swaps

tr:secmain.core:title: Forwards
tr:secmain.core:para: Forwards and additional leverage: Henderson on Derivatives [7.14]Like swaps, forwards (or forward rate agreements) are an agreement to exchange payment streams. They are a type of OTC derivative.
They are an arrangement between two parties which allows them to hedge against interest rate risk:

core:listitem.core:para: one party hedges against the risk of interest rates going down, and
the other party hedges against the risk of interest rates going up

tr:secmain.core:para: The parties agree to make a payment to the other at an agreed future settlement date based on the difference between the interest on a notional principal amount at a fixed rate of interest compared to interest on the same notional amount at a floating rate of interest. One party agrees to pay the fixed rate. The other party agrees to pay the floating rate.

core:listitem.core:para: if the fixed rate is higher than the floating rate, the party that agreed to pay the fixed rate pays a sum calculated on the basis of the difference between the fixed and floating rates to the other party

if the floating rate is higher than the fixed rate, the party that agreed to pay the floating rate pays a sum calculated on the basis of the difference between the fixed and floating rates to the other party

tr:secmain.core:title: Futures
tr:secmain.core:para: A future is a contract between two parties where one party agrees to buy and the other party agrees to sell an asset at a specific time in the future at a specific price. For example, futures are entered into in connection with the future price of a particular currency or commodity.

A futures contract is different from a commercial contract for sale where payment is made up front for the future delivery of goods. In a futures contract, the parties close out the transaction and settle up payments before the delivery date.

The terms of futures are standardised and as a result futures are only ever traded on exchanges. This is a key difference between a forward and a future as forwards are individually negotiated between the parties rather than being standardised. There is an active secondary market for futures because they are so standardised, whereas in contrast there is no secondary market for forwards.

tr:secmain.core:title: Options
tr:secmain.core:para: Options: Henderson on Derivatives [4.4]An option is a contract between two parties where one party agrees to buy and the other party agrees to sell an asset at a specific time in the future at a specific price. One party to the contract has the right to buy or sell the asset but is not obliged to do so. The party with the option pays a premium (ie a cash lump sum) to the other party.
A call option is where the party with the option to perform the contract is the buyer. A put option is where the party with the option to perform is the seller.
Options are traded on exchanges as well as OTC.

lnci:cite.lnci:content: The role of sustainability-linked derivatives in meeting global ESG goals (2021) 9 JIBFL 628

FCA regulation of interest rate hedging products: one rule for some? (2015) 7 JIBFL 425
Termination provisions of swap agreements II (2015) 2 JIBFL 83
